subject: maths
title: Probability
narrative: This rationalised NCERT chapter builds from conditional probability through the multiplication theorem, independence of events, the partition of a sample space and the theorem of total probability up to the capstone Bayes' theorem (reverse / a posteriori probability). The notion of a random variable is also introduced. Every node carries a clean verbatim source statement; the deterministic gates use the worked numeric examples of the chapter.

nodes.title: Compute a conditional probability
nodes.brief: Find P(E|F), the probability of E given that F has occurred, as P(E and F) / P(F).
nodes.quote: the conditional probability of the event E given that F has occurred, i.e. P (EIF) is given by
nodes.explanation: If E and F are two events of the same sample space, the conditional probability of E given that F has occurred, P(E|F), is P(E intersect F) / P(F), provided P(F) is not 0. It re-scopes the sample space to the outcomes favourable to F. For example, if P(A)=7/13, P(B)=9/13 and P(A and B)=4/13, then P(A|B) = (4/13)/(9/13) = 4/9.
keyMoves: Identify the conditioning event F and check P(F) is not 0
Compute P(E intersect F) and P(F)
Divide: P(E|F) = P(E intersect F) / P(F)
misconceptions: Dividing by P(E) instead of P(F)
Applying the formula when P(F) = 0

nodes.title: Use the complement property of conditional probability
nodes.explanation: Among the properties of conditional probability, Property 3 states that P(E'|F) = 1 - P(E|F): conditioning on F, the event E and its complement E' still have conditional probabilities summing to 1. This follows because P(S|F) = 1 and E, E' are disjoint with union S. It lets you find the conditional probability of a complement without recomputing from scratch.
keyMoves: Compute or recall P(E|F)
Subtract from 1 to get P(E'|F)
Use the fact that P(S|F) = 1 to justify it
misconceptions: Writing P(E'|F) = 1 - P(F|E) (wrong conditioning order)
Forgetting the conditioning event F stays the same on both sides

nodes.title: Apply the multiplication rule of probability
nodes.brief: Use P(E and F) = P(E) P(F|E) = P(F) P(E|F) to find the probability of a simultaneous occurrence.
nodes.quote: The above result is known as the multiplication rule of probability.
nodes.explanation: Rearranging the conditional-probability formula gives the multiplication rule of probability: P(E intersect F) = P(E) P(F|E) = P(F) P(E|F), provided P(E) and P(F) are non-zero. It finds the probability that both events occur. For three events it extends to P(E intersect F intersect G) = P(E) P(F|E) P(G|EF). For example, drawing two black balls without replacement from 10 black and 5 white gives (10/15)(9/14) = 3/7.
keyMoves: Identify the order in which the events occur
Multiply P(first) by the conditional probability of the next given the first
Extend the product for three or more events
misconceptions: Multiplying unconditional probabilities when the events are dependent
Forgetting to update the conditional probability after the first event (e.g. without replacement)

nodes.title: Test two events for independence
nodes.brief: Recognise E and F as independent when P(E and F) = P(E) P(F).
nodes.quote: Let E and F be two events associated with the same random experiment, then E and F are said to be independent if
nodes.explanation: Two events E and F associated with the same random experiment are independent if P(E intersect F) = P(E) P(F); equivalently P(E|F) = P(E) and P(F|E) = P(F), so the occurrence of one does not affect the probability of the other. To test independence, compute P(E intersect F) and compare it with the product P(E) P(F). They are dependent if these differ. Independence is not the same as being mutually exclusive.
keyMoves: Compute P(E), P(F) and P(E intersect F)
Check whether P(E intersect F) = P(E) P(F)
Conclude independent if equal, dependent if not
misconceptions: Confusing independent events with mutually exclusive events
Concluding independence from P(E|F) alone without checking the product rule

nodes.title: Test three events for mutual independence
nodes.brief: Check that all pairwise products and the triple product equal the corresponding intersection probabilities.
nodes.quote: Three events A, B and C are said to be mutually independent, if
nodes.explanation: Three events A, B and C are said to be mutually independent if all four conditions hold: P(A intersect B) = P(A)P(B), P(A intersect C) = P(A)P(C), P(B intersect C) = P(B)P(C), and P(A intersect B intersect C) = P(A)P(B)P(C). If at least one of these is not true, the events are not (mutually) independent. Pairwise independence alone is not enough; the triple-product condition must also hold.
keyMoves: Verify each of the three pairwise product conditions
Verify the triple-product condition P(A and B and C) = P(A)P(B)P(C)
Declare mutual independence only if all four hold
misconceptions: Concluding mutual independence from pairwise independence alone
Forgetting the triple-product condition

nodes.title: Recognise a partition of the sample space
nodes.brief: Identify events E1..En as a partition: pairwise disjoint, exhaustive, with non-zero probability.
nodes.quote: the events  $E_1, E_2, ..., E_n$  represent a partition of the sample space S if they are pairwise disjoint, exhaustive and have nonzero probabilities.
nodes.explanation: A set of events E1, E2, ..., En represents a partition of the sample space S if they are pairwise disjoint (Ei intersect Ej = empty for i not equal j), exhaustive (their union is S) and have non-zero probabilities. For example, any non-empty event E and its complement E' form a partition. Partitions are the setting for the theorem of total probability and Bayes' theorem.
keyMoves: Check the events are pairwise disjoint
Check their union is the whole sample space S
Check each has non-zero probability
misconceptions: Allowing overlapping events in a partition
Forgetting the events must cover the entire sample space

nodes.title: Apply the theorem of total probability
nodes.brief: Compute P(A) as the sum of P(Ej) P(A|Ej) over a partition E1..En.
nodes.quote: Let A be any event associated with S, then
nodes.explanation: If {E1, E2, ..., En} is a partition of the sample space S, each with non-zero probability, and A is any event associated with S, then by the theorem of total probability P(A) = P(E1)P(A|E1) + P(E2)P(A|E2) + ... + P(En)P(A|En) = sum over j of P(Ej)P(A|Ej). It assembles P(A) by conditioning on which part of the partition occurs. For example, with P(B)=0.65, P(A|B)=0.32, P(A|B')=0.80, P(A) = 0.65*0.32 + 0.35*0.80 = 0.488.
keyMoves: Identify a partition E1..En of the sample space
Find P(Ej) and the conditional P(A|Ej) for each part
Sum the products P(Ej)P(A|Ej) to get P(A)
misconceptions: Forgetting one part of the partition (sum no longer equals P(A))
Using P(Ej|A) instead of P(A|Ej) in the sum

nodes.title: Define a random variable
nodes.brief: Recognise a random variable as a real valued function on the sample space of a random experiment.
nodes.quote: A random variable is a real valued function whose domain is the sample space of a random experiment.
nodes.explanation: A random variable is a real valued function whose domain is the sample space of a random experiment: it assigns a real number to each outcome. For tossing a coin twice, S = {HH, HT, TH, TT}, and X = number of heads gives X(HH)=2, X(HT)=1, X(TH)=1, X(TT)=0. More than one random variable can be defined on the same sample space (e.g. Y = heads minus tails).
keyMoves: Take the sample space of the experiment as the domain
Assign a real number to each outcome by a rule
Recognise this rule as a random variable
misconceptions: Thinking a random variable is itself a probability (it is a function on outcomes)
Believing only one random variable can be defined on a sample space

nodes.title: Find the mean and variance of a discrete probability distribution
nodes.brief: Compute E(X) = Σ xᵢ pᵢ as the expected value, then Var(X) = E(X²) − [E(X)]² using the shortcut formula E(X²) = Σ xᵢ² pᵢ.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — The mean (expected value) E(X).
For a discrete random variable X taking values x₁, x₂, …, xₙ with probabilities p₁, p₂, …, pₙ (where Σpᵢ=1), the mean or expected value is E(X) = Σ xᵢ pᵢ. It is the probability-weighted average of all possible values. For X ∈ {1,2,3,4,5} each with probability 1/5: E(X) = (1+2+3+4+5)/5 = 3.

PART 2 — The second moment E(X²) and variance Var(X).
The variance measures spread: Var(X) = E(X²) − [E(X)]², where E(X²) = Σ xᵢ² pᵢ. For the same uniform distribution: E(X²) = (1+4+9+16+25)/5 = 11. So Var(X) = 11 − 9 = 2. The standard deviation is σ = √Var(X). This shortcut formula avoids computing Σ(xᵢ−μ)²pᵢ directly.

PART 3 — Using a distribution on a straight line (JEE pattern).
A common JEE setup: the points (x, P(X=x)) lie on a line, so P(X=x) = a + bx. Constraints Σpᵢ = 1 and Σxᵢpᵢ = given mean form a 2×2 linear system to solve for a and b; then use E(X²) = Σx²(a+bx) and Var = E(X²) − (mean)².
keyMoves: Compute E(X) = Σ xᵢ pᵢ (weighted average of values by probabilities)
Compute E(X²) = Σ xᵢ² pᵢ, then Var(X) = E(X²) − [E(X)]²
When probabilities lie on a line, set up and solve the system Σp=1, Σxp=mean to find the distribution
misconceptions: Computing Var(X) as E(X²) instead of E(X²) − [E(X)]²
Forgetting to square the mean in the shortcut: writing Var = E(X²) − E(X) (not squared)

nodes.title: Apply Bayes' theorem to find a reverse probability
nodes.brief: Use Bayes' theorem to find P(Ei|A) from the priors P(Ei) and the likelihoods P(A|Ei).
nodes.quote: Bayes' theorem is also called the formula for the probability of
nodes.explanation: Bayes' theorem solves the reverse-probability problem: given a partition E1..En of S and an event A of non-zero probability, P(Ei|A) = P(Ei)P(A|Ei) / sum over j of P(Ej)P(A|Ej). The Ei are the hypotheses, P(Ei) the prior (priori) probabilities and P(Ei|A) the posterior (a posteriori) probabilities; Bayes' theorem is also called the formula for the probability of causes. Its denominator is exactly the theorem of total probability. For example, with two bags it gives the chance a red ball came from Bag II; in the HIV-test example it gives P(actually HIV | tested positive) = 90/1089 ≈ 0.083.
keyMoves: Set up the partition (hypotheses) and their prior probabilities P(Ei)
Find the likelihoods P(A|Ei) and form the denominator by total probability
Apply P(Ei|A) = P(Ei)P(A|Ei) / sum P(Ej)P(A|Ej)
misconceptions: Confusing the prior P(Ei) with the posterior P(Ei|A)
Swapping P(A|Ei) and P(Ei|A) in the formula
